text: Relay K1 is a 24VDC control relay with one normally-open and one normally-closed contact. Coil terminals are A1 and A2. The NO contact is rated 10A resistive at 250VAC.
K2 is an ice-cube style plug-in relay mounted in an 11-pin socket on the DIN rail. Pins 2 and 10 are the coil; pins 1, 4, 5, 8, 9, 12 are the contact set.
Relay coils should be suppressed with a flyback diode or RC snubber when switched by a PLC output, to protect the transistor output from inductive kickback.
K3 is a control relay used to seal in the start pushbutton circuit. Its normally-open auxiliary contact holds the coil energized after the pushbutton is released.
A latching relay retains its last switched state without continuous coil power, useful for maintaining an alarm state through a momentary power loss.
Relay contact ratings de-rate with inductive loads; a 10A resistive contact may only safely switch 2-3A on a small motor or solenoid load.
The GS10 variable frequency drive accepts a 0-10V or 4-20mA speed reference on terminals VR and ACM, and a run-forward contact closure on terminal FWD.
VFD parameter P09.03 sets the Modbus communication timeout; if no valid frame is received within that window the drive trips a communication fault.
PowerFlex 525 drives display fault codes on the integrated keypad; for example F0004 indicates an output ground fault detected during a run command.
A VFD's DC bus voltage should read approximately 320V when idle on a 230V single-phase input, per the bench baseline recorded for this drive.
Drive acceleration and deceleration ramp times are set independently; a short decel ramp on a high-inertia load can trip an overvoltage fault.
Skip frequencies are programmed into a VFD to avoid running a motor continuously at a resonant frequency that causes mechanical vibration.
An inductive proximity switch detects metal targets without contact; a PNP output sources current to the load when the target is sensed.
NPN proximity sensors sink current to common when triggered, and require a pull-up resistor on the PLC input if the input card is not a sourcing type.
Sensing distance for an 18mm inductive proximity switch is typically 5-8mm for mild steel, and less for non-ferrous metals like aluminum.
A normally-open proximity switch outputs 0V (or open) with no target present, and switches to the supply voltage when the target enters range.
Proximity switches used for conveyor jam detection are mounted so the sensing face is perpendicular to the product's direction of travel.
Capacitive proximity sensors can detect non-metallic targets like plastic or liquid level, unlike inductive sensors which require a metal target.
Terminal strip X4 carries the field wiring between the PLC I/O card and the panel-mounted pushbuttons; each terminal is numbered to match the wire number.
Wire 51 runs from relay K1 terminal A1 to terminal block X4 position 17, carrying the 24VDC control signal for the start circuit.
Ground terminal blocks are bonded to the panel backplate and should show continuity to earth ground of less than 1 ohm.
Fused terminal blocks combine a disconnect and a fuse holder in one DIN-rail terminal, simplifying field-wiring troubleshooting.
Terminal block X3 position 9 carries the fused 120VAC control power distributed to the pilot lights and selector switches mounted on the door.
Double-level terminal blocks stack two independent circuits in the same DIN-rail footprint, saving panel space on dense wiring jobs.
Cross-reference numbers printed above a terminal block point to the sheet and grid location where the other end of that wire is drawn.
